J1.basis: "InboxZap (rebrand in progress; legacy brand 'Lumina' — AI meeting assistant — still live on production)" and "finish the pivot consistently — homepage/nav/footer copy... all need to describe InboxZap"
J1.rationale: The entrant richly identifies two identities and cites data throughout, but inverts the pivot: it treats the live meeting-assistant product as the stale legacy to overwrite and the inbox-cleanup GSC history as the go-forward target market — the reverse of the intended read, privileging a head-tag edit over the entire live product body/nav/JSON-LD it itself observed is 100% meeting-assistant.

J2.basis: "post-1... Evidence (GSC): 3900 impressions, 2 clicks, avg position 42.3 ... Action: Refresh/repurpose ... post-7 ... 80 impressions ... 55.8" — every one of 13 assets gets a disposition with cited impressions/clicks/position
J2.rationale: Genuinely walks the full legacy inventory asset-by-asset with real GSC numbers and an explicit disposition (12 harvest / 1 redirect / 0 sunset) plus reasoning, strongly anchor-10 in form; docked because the topical-fit signal is a flat mechanical 50% for every asset (so the all-harvest outcome isn't truly differentiated) and rests on the inverted pivot premise.

J3.basis: "All 12 legacy blog assets are on-topic for the new direction and were harvested in place ... content being progressively refreshed" plus the roadmap sequencing posts by impression volume
J3.rationale: It avoids the torch-everything mode (keeps equity, adds a 301, sequences a transition), but lands the doubling-down failure — building the growth engine around inbox-cleanup content for the product the fixture says pivoted away from it, rather than treating that demand as legacy to bridge.

J1.basis: "Finish the pivot consistently — homepage/nav/footer copy, /features, /pricing, and JSON-LD all need to describe InboxZap" and "real searchers already associate this domain with the new brand [InboxZap]"
J1.rationale: The entrant noticed the head/body mismatch and that the meeting-assistant content is live, so it isn't blind to a pivot — but it inverted the direction: it treated the coherent live Lumina meeting-assistant site as the 'stale' state and InboxZap inbox-cleanup as the new product, then rewrote the whole site around the legacy inbox demand. That is precisely anchor_0's failure (building the plan around ranking InboxZap-era content harder).

J2.basis: "13 legacy assets classified: 12 harvest · 1 redirect · 0 sunset" with per-asset lines like "blog/post-1 — 3900 impressions, 2 clicks, avg position 42.3 ... Bridgeable — keep the equity"
J2.rationale: Structurally this nails anchor_10 — asset-by-asset dispositions citing real GSC impressions/positions with stated actions — but the reasoning is boilerplate-identical ('relevance 50% ... harvest') for all 12, and the dispositions are anchored to the inverted direction, so the substantive calls (harvest all inbox posts, redirect/rewrite the meeting-assistant home away) are largely wrong even though the form is disciplined.

J3.basis: "All 12 blog posts are on-topic for the new direction already and should be refreshed in place (not replaced)" and homepage "harvested (rewritten in place)" into InboxZap
J3.rationale: The entrant fell squarely into one failure mode: it doubled down on the legacy inbox-cleanup corpus as the growth engine and overwrote the current meeting-assistant positioning (rewriting the live homepage/JSON-LD to inbox cleanup). It preserved 'equity' but toward the wrong pole, with no bridge for the actual current product — anchor_0's 'doubles down on inbox-cleanup content for a product that no longer exists.'

J1.basis: "The company is pivoting from its previous product, Lumina (an AI meeting-notes assistant)" ... "All 12 blog posts are on-topic for the new direction already and should be refreshed in place"
J1.rationale: The entrant correctly observed the live site is a meeting assistant, but drew the inverted conclusion — treating the meeting-assistant identity as the legacy state and inbox-cleanup (InboxZap) as the current target market, then rewriting the live product accordingly. GSC's inbox-cleanup history is the historical/legacy signal, yet the entrant made it the destination, so the core intent inference is backwards.

J2.basis: "post-1 ... 3900 impressions, 2 clicks, avg position 42.3 ... Bridgeable — keep the equity. Action: Refresh/repurpose ... (keep the URL)" — repeated asset-by-asset for all 13 with GSC numbers and a disposition
J2.rationale: Methodologically this matches anchor_10: it walks the legacy inventory asset-by-asset, cites real impressions/clicks/position from the export, and assigns each an explicit disposition (12 harvest, 1 redirect, 0 sunset) with reasoning. Weakened by a uniform mechanical '50% relevance, bridgeable' verdict on every post and a nonsensical 0%-relevance homepage-redirect flag, but the per-asset, data-grounded disposition work is genuinely present.

J3.basis: "finish the pivot consistently — homepage/nav/footer copy, /features, /pricing, and JSON-LD all need to describe InboxZap" and "12 harvest ... All 12 legacy blog assets are on-topic for the new direction and were harvested in place"
J3.rationale: Rather than balancing the two failure modes, the entrant fell squarely into one — doubling down on inbox-cleanup content and actively overwriting the current live meeting-assistant product into inbox-cleanup. It does sequence a transition and preserve equity, but aimed at the wrong positioning, so there is no defensible middle path.
